---
title: "Varieties as Conditioning Structure"
subtitle: "A Game-Theoretic and Bayesian Framework"
author: "Brett Reynolds"
institute: "Humber Polytechnic & University of Toronto"
date: "LVC Study Group -- 27 March 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../../.house-style/quarto-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    hash-type: number
    center: false
    transition: none
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.06
    self-contained: true
---

## A syntactician walks into a study group...

Most of my work has been in English syntax, not LVC, but recently I've been working on some ideas from the philosophy of science that I wanted to share here -- LVC is so important to understanding syntax, and it's a good test case for these ideas. I want to know if this is useful to you or if I'm missing something obvious.

::: {.notes}
Set expectations. This grew out of my HPC book project, where I needed a principled account of how variety categories work. I'm bringing tools from philosophy of science -- game theory and Bayesian learning -- to concepts you use every day. I want genuine pushback from people who know variation better than I do.
:::

## The claim

Register, dialect, and discourse community are three **anchors** for the same judgment.

The framework: varieties as **conditioning structure** -- stable patterns in what forms are appropriate given what.

The learning mechanism is Bayesian: speakers track which forms go with which circumstances and update with experience.

::: {.notes}
The core claim. The standard move in sociolinguistics is to define variety types by external correlates: dialects are geographic or social, registers are functional or situational. But these criteria often conflict. I'm proposing we ask a different question: what is appropriateness indexed to? A note on "appropriateness": I'm using this as a model-internal term for what listeners compute, not as a normative claim that any form is actually inappropriate. The whole point of the framework is to make visible how those judgments produce inequity.
:::

## Two sources

**Wiese (2023):** Grammar lives in [communicative situations]{.term}, not named languages. Varieties describe regularities across situations.

**O'Connor (2019):** Linguistic choices solve [coordination problems]{.term} along two payoff dimensions: communicative clarity (rewards convergence) and identity management (rewards divergence).

When the two align, clusters tighten. When they conflict: code-switching, style-shifting, register mixing.

::: {.notes}
Two theoretical pillars. Wiese provides the ontology: the primary unit is the communicative situation, not the named language. O'Connor provides the dynamics: communities evolve categories to coordinate action, and the categories track payoff similarity -- what matters for successful interaction -- not objective feature similarity. Her sim-max signalling game framework shows that stable partitions (Voronoi languages) emerge under evolutionary dynamics, and that the boundaries are shaped by the community's functional needs, not by objective property clustering. The two payoff dimensions -- communicative clarity vs. identity management -- predict exactly where boundary phenomena should appear.
:::

## The three anchors {#anchors}

| Anchor | Conditions on | Traditional label |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|
| [Situation]{.term} | Activity, setting, goals | Register |
| [Ascription]{.term} | How you're classified | Dialect |
| [Identification]{.term} | Whose norms you orient to | Discourse community |

Same judgment type. Different conditioning variable.

::: {.notes}
Here's the framework laid out. All three are dimensions of the same thing -- appropriateness. They differ in what that appropriateness is indexed to. Situation is token-level -- it shifts within a day, within a conversation, sometimes within an utterance. Ascription is speaker-level -- durable, tracking features others treat as stable. Ascriptions can be contested and may shift over biographical time, but they have material consequences because others condition their behaviour on them. Identification is trajectory-level -- you can change who you orient to, but such changes are effortful and socially consequential.

People who know the three-waves literature better than I do may see a mapping here -- ascription aligning roughly with first-wave demographic conditioning, identification with third-wave attention to social meaning and stance. I'd be curious whether that mapping holds up or oversimplifies.
:::

## The interesting space

::: {.r-fit-text}
Ascription and identification can **align** -- or they can **diverge**.
:::

When they align: forms appropriate to your ascribed variety also signal your identification. No tension.

When they diverge: you're navigating between how you're heard and who you're orienting to.

**That gap is where style-shifting, accommodation, and "passing" live.**

::: {.notes}
This is the key insight. A working-class kid in law school has an ascribed position (class background, regional origin) but identifies with a new community (legal profession). Their speech reflects both: the ascribed baseline modulated by the identificational target. The gap between ascription and identification is a major locus of sociolinguistic agency.
:::

## A case: ascription overrides identification

I lived in Japan for ten years. Japanese spouse, bilingual children.

In restaurants, wait staff would ask my wife what I wanted. I'd reply in Japanese. They'd follow up with her.

. . .

My **identification** was fully oriented to the Japanese-speaking community. My **ascription** overrode the signal. The listener couldn't update on the evidence because ascription was doing all the conditioning work.

::: {.notes}
This is the ascription-identification gap made personal. My identification history was thoroughly Japanese-oriented: decade of residence, Japanese family, daily life in Japanese. But ascription -- how listeners classified me on sight -- was so strong it blocked the uptake of the actual linguistic signal. This connects to what critical sociolinguists call raciolinguistic perception: the perception is prior to the signal. The framework gives that insight a formal address rather than treating it as an embarrassment for the model.
:::

## Identification shapes production

I learned much of my Japanese from women -- friends, students, my wife. My default first person is [boku]{.mention}, not [ore]{.mention}. I never acquired the particle [sa]{.mention}. I tend to be more casual than the situation might warrant.

. . .

My production reflects whose norms I tracked -- that's the prior doing its work.

The same values show up in English: I tell my EAP students to call me "Brett," not "Professor Reynolds." Identification can be consistent across languages even when ascription differs radically.

::: {.notes}
Two layers here. First, identification history shapes production distributions. My Japanese reflects whose norms I tracked during acquisition -- the women I learned from had different distributions from the stereotypical male speaker. Second, the anti-hierarchy preference operates across languages: same values, different ascriptive contexts. Identification encompasses whose values you're enacting, not only whose community you're in. And both layers collide with ascription: someone producing boku-inflected, casually-registered Japanese gets heard through the foreigner filter before any of these nuances land.
:::

## All three matter at once

The full model conditions on situation, ascription, and identification together.

- Hold A and I constant, vary S: you see **register** variation
- Hold S and I constant, vary A: you see **dialect** variation
- Hold S and A constant, vary I: you see **identification** effects

Register, dialect, and discourse community are slices of one joint distribution, not three separate models. Learners track this joint distribution and update with experience.

::: {.notes}
The three dimensions interact. The interesting theoretical claims (the ascription-identification gap, style-shifting as navigation) live in the interactions. For quantitative work, this means tokens from the same speaker aren't independent; speakers with similar ascriptions share structure; situational effects operate within these nested levels. The Bayesian point is just that learners track which forms go with which S/A/I configurations and update. That's what happened to me in Japan -- I acquired a particular joint distribution from a particular set of people in particular situations. I have backup slides with the formal notation if anyone wants to see the model written out.

The metaphor I use in the book: imagine two bolt factories, each internally precise. Dump both bins together and it looks noisy. The process isn't noisy; the analysis failed to condition on source. One pooled curve isn't one grammar.
:::

## Data: conditioning in action

**Double copula:** [The thing is, is that...]{.mention} Conditioned, not random: clusters with focus-marking contexts and certain subject nouns. First identified by Janda (1980); Coppock (2018) analyses it as a focus-marking device.

**Existential [there's]{.mention}:** Three variants (Krejci & Hilton 2022). Invariant [there's]{.mention} has its own distributional profile -- stable in casual speech, near-absent in edited prose.

**Service-encounter reflexive:** [And yourself?]{.mention} No style guide teaches it; prescriptive pressure can't kill it because the social reward outweighs the grammatical cost. Convergence, not instruction.

::: {.notes}
Three cases where what looks like noise or error at the pooled level turns out to be tight structure at the conditioned level. The double copula isn't random hesitation -- it clusters predictably. Existential there's isn't agreement failure -- it's a different variant with its own distribution. The service-encounter reflexive isn't a mistake -- it indexes deference and attentive service in a way that plain "you" doesn't, and new servers acquire it through convergence, not training. Each case shows varieties as conditioning structure: condition on the right variables and the noise resolves into signal.
:::

## Indexicality as inverse conditioning

If certain forms are more likely in certain contexts, then hearing a form tells you something about the context.

That's just Bayes' theorem running in reverse.

- Hearing [ore]{.mention} suggests a casual, male-peer **situation**
- Hearing foreign-accented speech suggests a non-native **ascription**
- Choosing [boku]{.mention} over [ore]{.mention} claims alignment with a particular **identification** network

**Social meaning is the informational flip-side of production conditioning.**

::: {.notes}
Indexicality falls out of the conditioning framework directly. If speakers condition form choice on S/A/I, listeners can invert the conditioning to infer S/A/I from the form. The Japanese first-person pronoun system is a good example because the same form carries situational, ascriptive, and identificational information simultaneously. A single variant supports a field of partially compatible inferences rather than a one-to-one mapping -- context selects which region of that field is foregrounded.
:::

## Anchoring can shift

All three anchors are always present. What shifts is which one dominates.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Quotative [like]{.mention}**

|  | Early | Mid | Now |
|--|-------|-----|-----|
| **S** | weak | strong | residual |
| **A** | weak | spreading | general |
| **I** | **dominant** | fading | negligible |
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Japanese [だよね]{.mention}**

|  | Pre-1990s | 1990s | Now |
|--|-----------|-------|-----|
| **S** | weak | weak | **dominant** |
| **A** | **dominant** | contested | weak |
| **I** | default | **dominant** | weak |
:::

::::

The dominant anchor shifted -- in opposite directions.

::: {.notes}
These two cases show opposite trajectories through the same S/A/I space. Quotative like moved from identification to situation to ascription -- diffusion outward from an in-group. Japanese だよね moved from ascriptive norm (gendered particle ideology) through noticed violation to situational neutralisation. The framework doesn't privilege any particular trajectory; it just says anchoring can shift. Okamoto (1995) documents the broader pattern of young Japanese women adopting "moderately masculine" sentence-final forms, of which だよね is a prime example. I was living in Japan during this shift and remember the DA.YO.NE song making the form culturally hyper-salient.
:::

## What this reframes

| Old framing | New framing |
|-------------|-------------|
| Code-switching between X and Y | Shift in conditioning anchor |
| Style as individual expression | Coordination with a reference group |
| Dialect as property of speaker | Ascription by listeners |
| Register as property of situation | What situation licenses |

The categories don't disappear. They get reanalysed as dimensions of conditioning.

::: {.notes}
I'm not dissolving the categories. Register, dialect, and discourse community remain useful working concepts -- they pick out real patterns that matter for sampling, stratification, and analysis. What the framework adds is a principled account of what makes them different. If you're studying regional variation, you're conditioning on ascription. If you're studying style-shifting, you're looking at how situation modulates an ascribed baseline. If you're studying professional socialisation, you're tracking how identification shifts. The framework tells you what you're doing when you make these distinctions.
:::

## Agency

::: {.r-fit-text}
Ascription is imposed. Identification is (partially) chosen.
:::

The gap between them is a major locus of speaker agency.

My casual Japanese register and my "call me Brett" are the same move: identification pushing back against ascription-based hierarchy, in two different languages.

::: {.notes}
My casualness in Japanese reflects the same egalitarian values I enact in English, not just a proficiency artefact. The ascription differs radically (white foreigner in Japan, white professor in Toronto), but the identification is consistent: orient away from hierarchy. The framework captures this cross-linguistic consistency because identification is about values and normative orientation, not just community membership.
:::

## The dark side

A listener hears a non-standard form and something shifts -- in expectations, evaluation, behaviour.

When everyone makes similar judgments, they become **coordination signals**.

"Standard" vs "non-standard" = stable equilibrium. Self-sustaining. Hard to exit unilaterally.

. . .

But equilibria respond to structural change. Changing what's conditioned on (anonymising, removing signals) shifts the equilibrium. Changing attitudes alone doesn't.

::: {.notes}
I'm not telling you that standardisation is unjust -- you know that better than I do. What I'm offering is a formal account of why it's self-sustaining. In O'Connor's framework, once "standard" and "non-standard" become salient categories, they function as coordination signals. Employers coordinate on using "standard" speech as a hiring filter -- not because any individual employer decided to discriminate, but because everyone expects everyone else to use this signal. The equilibrium is self-sustaining. Even employers who know the linguistic facts face pressure to conform.

A caveat: the categories that structure this coordination have deep histories. The prestige hierarchy was built by colonialism, state formation, mass education. The game-theoretic point is that once these categories are salient, they sustain themselves through coordination -- but the framework doesn't explain how they got salient. History does that. Ascription categories are historically produced, not just inputs to a model.

This validates what critical sociolinguists have argued: individual attitude change is insufficient without structural change. We can keep teaching Standard varieties to those who want to learn them -- offering repertoire expansion is consistent with valuing identification. The problem is conditioning access on already having it.
:::

## What this predicts

The framework generates testable claims:

1. **Conditioned models outperform pooled.** Models that condition on S/A/I should outperform pooled baselines on out-of-sample prediction. No conditioning gain, no maintenance claim.
2. **Anchor-shift trajectories are ordered.** Forms spreading outward should develop situational conditioning before approaching ascription-like durability.
3. **Weaken a stabiliser, the category degrades.** Milroy's Belfast work: network dissolution led to dialect levelling. That's the prediction confirmed.

::: {.notes}
These are predictions that distinguish this framework from alternative accounts. P28 from the book: conditioned models should outperform pooled baselines -- and the gain measures the contribution of social maintenance. The anchor-shift prediction can be tested against corpus data. Belfast is a confirmed instance of the general HPC prediction: weaken a stabiliser and the category should degrade in identifiable ways. Each prediction is specific enough to be falsifiable. "No conditioning gain, no maintenance claim" is the methodological commitment: report pooled and conditioned models side by side, test whether projectibility returns under conditioning, and state what result would count against your analysis.
:::

## Discussion {.center}

Does the S/A/I distinction help with phenomena you work on?

Where does it break?

What predictions does it make about your data that alternative frameworks don't?

::: {.notes}
Three specific provocations. First: is this useful? Does it clarify things you're already studying, or is it just relabelling? Second: where does it fail? Cases where the three dimensions can't be pulled apart, or where the framework makes wrong predictions. Third: the hardest question. If someone can show me a case where the framework makes the same predictions as existing accounts, it's not earning its keep. I want to know where it generates novel expectations -- and especially where those expectations might be wrong.

Also a methodological challenge I'd like help with: situation is tractable (code it from observable features), ascription is harder but doable (group by region, class, etc.), but identification is the challenge. If identification is inferred from the very linguistic choices being modelled, the analysis is circular. Independent measures are needed: network structure, self-report, stance markers, participation histories. What would that look like in your work?
:::

## Backup: formal notation {visibility="hidden"}

The joint distribution over forms, conditioned on all three anchors:

$$Y \mid S{=}s,\, A{=}a,\, I{=}i \;\sim\; \text{Categorical}(\theta_{s,a,i})$$

Marginalise over A and I to recover register variation. Marginalise over S and I to recover dialect variation. The three "variety types" are slices of one joint distribution.

::: {.notes}
Backup slide for anyone who wants the formal notation. The key point is that the three dimensions interact -- you need the joint distribution, not three independent marginals. This is why the framework predicts specific distributional signatures for speakers whose ascription and identification diverge: their production reflects the joint conditioning, not a simple switch between two marginal distributions.
:::
